phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, pouvez-vous m'indiquer la section cent douze ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-KYOO-zay mwah, poo-VAY voo man-dee-KAY lah sek-SYON sahn DOOZ ?
phrases.cultural_note: French uses a 24-style number system — 112 is 'cent douze' (one hundred twelve). Worth practicing large numbers before attending a big event!

responses.french: La section cent douze est au niveau inférieur, à votre gauche.
responses.english: Section 112 is on the lower level, to your left.
responses.phonetic: lah sek-SYON sahn DOOZ ay oh nee-VOH an-fay-RYUR, ah votr GOHSH.

phrases.intent_en: Order a pretzel at the concession stand
phrases.text_local: Un bretzel, s'il vous plaît. Avec de la moutarde ?
phrases.pronunciation: un bret-ZEL, seel voo PLAY. ah-VEK duh lah moo-TARD ?
phrases.cultural_note: The pretzel is a beloved snack in Alsace, the French region bordering Germany — so it's not entirely un-French to crave one at a game.

responses.french: Oui, on a de la moutarde. Ça fait quatre dollars.
responses.english: Yes, we have mustard. That's four dollars.
responses.phonetic: WEE, on ah duh lah moo-TARD. sah fay KAT-ruh dol-AR.

responses.french: Désolé, il n'y en a plus. On a des nachos.
responses.english: Sorry, we're out. We have nachos.
responses.phonetic: day-zo-LAY, eel nyahn ah PLOO. on ah day NAH-chos.

phrases.cultural_note: Kentucky basketball is a religion in Lexington — locals will warmly appreciate any visitor joining in the cheer, in any language.
'Mi-temps' literally means 'half-time' and is used in all French team sports. It's also used figuratively — 'je suis à la mi-temps de ma carrière' means 'I'm at the midpoint of my career.'

responses.french: La mi-temps commence dans deux minutes.
responses.phonetic: lah mee-TAHN koh-MAHNS dahn duh mee-NOOT.

phrases.cultural_note: French sports commentary loves dramatic superlatives — 'impressionnant,' 'spectaculaire,' 'incroyable' are all staples. Lean into the enthusiasm.

phrases.intent_en: Politely ask someone in front of you to sit down
phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, pourriez-vous vous asseoir ? Je ne vois rien.
phrases.pronunciation: ex-KYOO-zay mwah, poor-YAY voo vooz ah-SWAHR ? zhuh nuh vwah RYEN.
phrases.cultural_note: The conditional 'pourriez-vous' (could you) is the polite way to make a request in French — much softer than the imperative 'asseyez-vous' (sit down), which could sound rude.

responses.phonetic: ex-KYOO-zay mwah, zhuh swee tel-MAHN ahn-bah-LAY !
